season_calendar.forecast: Mist in the low lane could clear into one silver-edged frost before market day.
Old pear bloom has bees working every warm hour between showers.
Three bright afternoons are likely, though the creek still cools the night air.
Rain keeps the lane soft; north porches smell like moss and wet stone.
Half the town swears one more frost is coming; the other half already planted tomatoes. Someone will be smug by Friday.
Every porch has a flat of starts on it, and every conversation opens with what went into the ground yesterday.
The mornings run long and gold now; beds dry out by noon and the bees are at work before the kettle is.
Children own the creek again. Tovan wants sturdy things settled before the school garden is left to the sprinkler's mercy.
No real rain in ten days. The creek is polite about it; the rain barrels are not.
Too hot to cook indoors, so nobody does. Supper moves to the porches and everyone judges the planters, kindly.
The valley holds its breath in the afternoons. Water early, shade the trays, and forgive anything that wilts by four.
One night cool enough for a blanket, and suddenly everyone remembers autumn exists and asks what keeps its color.
Ladders in the old rows, wasps drunk on windfalls, and every kitchen smelling of peeled apples by Thursday.
The school garden gets its second wind. Tovan needs plants that survive being loved by thirty children at once.
The orchards open their gates for gleaning. Bring a basket, and leave the low branches for shorter neighbors.
The lanes go soft and glad about it. Moss brightens on the north steps like it never left.
Fog hangs in the orchard rows and customers ask what will settle in before cold.
The real frost, not the gossip kind. Anything tender still outside is a lesson now.
Light is down to a ribbon between the hills. Windowsills become the whole garden, and are taken seriously.
Woodsmoke settles in the valley by four o'clock. People shop slowly, mostly to stand near other people.
The longest night of the year. Hush Arbor answers it the old way: something living and lit in every window.
Cold that creaks the porch boards. Nothing moves outside but chimney smoke, and nothing should.
The catalogs arrive damp from the mailboxes and are read like letters from someone missed all year.
The creek runs louder every day and the frost gossip changes direction: this time it's about how soon.

community_events.name: First Seed Swap
community_events.request: Bring sturdy starts with a story, label them honestly, and do not make newcomers feel small.
community_events.etiquette: Every contribution needs a plain care note and one useful local warning.
community_events.relationship_note: showed up for the seed-swap table with useful, honest labels

community_events.name: Porch Supper Week
community_events.request: Send plants that make a supper table friendlier — things to cook with, things to sit beside. Nothing that upstages the host.
community_events.etiquette: A supper plant arrives with a use: a recipe, a scent, or a story worth retelling.
community_events.relationship_note: sent plants that made porch suppers easier to host

community_events.name: Orchard Gleaning Week
community_events.request: The old rows open for gleaning. The orchard wants plants that give back to tired soil, from people who know what a windfall is worth.
community_events.etiquette: Take from the high branches, leave the low ones for shorter neighbors, and never comment on someone's basket.
community_events.relationship_note: helped gleaning week with plants that fed the soil back

community_events.name: Longest Night Lanterns
community_events.request: Every window wants something living and lit for the longest night. Bring what glows, what gifts well, or what carries a story worth the dark.
community_events.etiquette: A lantern plant is half light and half promise; label what it needs to still be alive in spring.
community_events.relationship_note: kept windows living and lit through the longest night

market_signals.text: Possible frost Thursday. Cover tender starts if the downslope wind clears the mist.
Good price on hardy greens and chives; lantern tomatoes arrived pretty but early.
Three people asked whether a porch can be beautiful if it only gets morning sun.
School garden workday moved up. Tovan needs sturdy plants with stories before the children lose interest.
The creek gauge reads low and the rain barrels are spoken for. Anything that shrugs at a dry week earns its bench space.
Mara's board says cold plates and herb-everything until the kitchen cools down. She buys what she can pick the same day.
The bee count is up in the old rows. The orchard association notices who sells pollinator plants, quietly.
The cider press schedule is posted and every kitchen within a mile is planning around it.
The town mulch pile opens Saturday. Serious gardeners are thinking about their soil now, not their flowers.
The windowsill club is comparing notes again. Small, forgiving, and good in low light wins the month.
A flyer for the lantern walk went up at the crossing. People want something living to carry; the glow is optional but remembered.
Cilla is running the gift-wrapping table this year, which means every plant that leaves the stand had better look intentional.

week_outcomes.text: The frost only silvered the creek grass, but customers remembered who warned them. Hardy trays sold before noon.
The north-porch crowd compared notes over violet starts. Cilla pretended not to be pleased when hers looked best.
Lantern tomatoes drew hands to the bench, then the cold made everyone ask for cover cloth. Pretty did not mean ready.
The rain held off, just like the creek gauge said. Whoever bought drought-shruggers watered nothing and told everyone.
Mara picked her order at dawn and the specials board did the advertising. Herb-everything, all week.
Gleaning baskets came back heavy and the tired rows got their mulch. The orchard association remembers who helped.
Every window on the lane held something alive on the longest night. Yours were the ones people slowed down for.
